narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70 and LSAT score of 20. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on students' academic and background details, estimated a 64% chance that you would fail it—high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your application. This decision hinged on just a few key factors from your record that carried the most weight in the model's assessment. The strongest influence pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers that year—far below the typical student's midpoint around the 5th or 6th decile—and signaled to the model a notable dip in performance at a critical stage. Next in influence was your LSAT score of 20, significantly lower than the average of about 37 across past students, further tilting the prediction toward bar failure by highlighting potential challenges with the rigorous reasoning demands of legal exams. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, while below the dataset average of 3.21, worked in your favor to some degree, pulling the prediction slightly back toward the chance of passing. Compared to typical applicants who succeed, your combination of lower decile rankings and LSAT score deviated more sharply from the stronger academic profiles that usually predict bar passage. While factors like your full-time status aligned with the vast majority of students (over 90%), they didn't outweigh these core academic signals. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many students with similar starts have improved dramatically—but the model prioritizes these predictors based on historical patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted study or additional coursework, and reapplying once you've addressed these gaps, as even modest improvements in these areas can shift predictions favorably for applicants like you.
